coredata.dc:title: Surface charge accumulation of functionalized carbonized polymer dots selectively induces lysosomal membrane permeabilization of breast cancer cells

coredata.dc:description: Lysosomal membrane permeabilization (LMP) has become an attractive strategy in tumor therapy. However, non-specific cytotoxicity caused by LMP remains a challenge; whether lysosomal-dependent autophagy process affected by lysosomal damage is still unclear. Here, we provide a carbonized polymer dots (CPDs)-based lysosomal targeted nano-strategy that mediates specific cytotoxicity to breast cancer cells by inducing LMP and disruption of autophagy degradation. CPDs functionalized via covalent conjugation with PpIX modified cathepsin D (CTSD) specific substrate peptide (CPDs-PP) were designed to achieve the selectivity to breast cancer cells. The effects and underlying mechanism of CPDs-PP on lysosomal membrane integrity and autophagic flux were determined. CPDs-PP with pH-dependent protonation and CTSD-responsive surface positive charge accumulation property at specific pH were obtained. Conversion of CPDs-PP to CPDs-P and ratiometric green–red fluorescence switching were observed in breast cancer cells with high expression of CTSD. It has been confirmed that CPDs-PP induced LMP and autophagic flux blockage of breast cancer cells. Lysosomal lipid accumulation was demonstrated to be the potential mechanism. CPDs-PP-induced LMP exhibited selective cytotoxicity to adherent tumor cells and tumor spheres, which was furtherly enhanced by spatiotemporal controlled lysosomal specific photodamage. This strategy offers promise for utilization of lysosome-targeting nanocarriers for anti-cancer pro-drugs and drug delivery systems.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CPDs. (A) TEM images of CPDs21:9, CPDs20:8, CPDs22:7 and CPDs-PP. (B, C) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s and O1s of CPDs21:9, CPDs20:8 and CPDs22:7, respectively. (D, E) Hydrodynamic diameter (DH) distributions and zeta potentials of CPDs21:9, CPDs20:8 and CPDs22:7 at different pH. (F) Schematic illustration of the structure of CPDs and the mechanism of pH-dependent aggregation and surface positive charge accumulation.

Ph-dependent CPDs-PP aggregation after CTSD digestion. (a) Schematic illustration of covalent conjugation between CPDs and PpIX via a CTSD-cleavable peptide linker to form CPDs-PP, that cleaved by CTSD to form CPDs-P. (B) Hydrodynamic diameters of CPDs-PP and CPDs-P at different pH from 7.5 to 4.5. (C) TEM images and diameters distribution histograms of CPDs-P at different pH from 7.5 to 4.5. (D) Zeta potentials of CPDs-PP and CPDs-P at different pH from 7.5 to 4.5. (E) Schematic diagram of the dispersity of CPDs-PP, and aggregation of CPDs-P in acidic environment.

FRET-based fluorescence conversion of CPDs-PP. (A) Normalized Fluorescence (FL) emission spectra upon excitation at 400 nm and UV–vis spectra of CPDs and PpIX. FL emission spectra of (B) CPDs21:8, P-PpIX, CPDs-PP and (C) CPDs-PP exposed to various concentrations of CTSD from 0.1 to 3.5 μg/mL upon excitation at 400 nm and fluorescence images under UV light of 365 nm. (D) Linear relationship between CTSD concentration and ratio of CPDs-PP FL intensity at 490 nm to 630 nm (F490 /F630). (E) FL emission spectra of CPDs21:8, CPDs-PP exposure to 10 μg/mL CTSD and CPDs-P. (F) Schematic diagram of FRET-based fluorescence conversion of CPDs-PP responded to CTSD. (G) FL emission spectra of CPDs-PP exposure to CTSD, CTSB, CTSL, CTSK, CTSS and CTSD plus its inhibitor, respectively. The concentration of each cathepsin was 1.5 μg/mL, and Pepstain A was 10.0 μg/mL.

Fluorescence images of MDA-MB-231, MCF-7 and MCF-10A cells incubated with CPDs-PP. (A) Western blot analysis of CTSD levels of MDA-MB-231, MCF-7 and MCF-10A cells. (B) Relative protein expression of CTSD in different cells normalized to TUBA. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3 per group). **p < 0.01. (C) Fluorescence and pseudo color images and (D) flow cytometry dot plots of MDA-MB-231, MCF-7 and MCF-10A cells incubated with 2.0 mg/mL of CPDs-PP with/without Pepstain A. Scale bars, 30 μm.

Distribution of CPDs-P in MDA-MB-231 and MCF-10A cells. (A) TEM images of cells and enlarged lysosomes incubated with CPDs-PP. CPDs-P are indicated by red arrows. (B) Fluorescence images of cells and enlarged lysosomes incubated with CPDs-PP for 2.5 h, and labeled with LysoBrite Blue for lysosomes. The swelled lysosomes are indicated by red arrows. (C) Schematic diagram of lysosomal expansion due to the proton sponge effect of CPDs-P. (D) Fluorescence images of cells incubated with CPDs-PP for 3, 6 and 12 h, and labeled with LysoBrite Blue for lysosomes. Cytoplasmic green fluorescence is indicated by yellow arrows. Scale bars for small and enlarged images: 30 µm and 10 µm, respectively. (D) Pearson correlation coefficient (Pr) of relative spatial distribution between CPDs-P and lysosome. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Effects of CPDs-P on lysosomal membrane integrity. (A) AO staining images and (B) Normalized cytoplasmic green and lysosomal red FL of MDA-MB-231, MCF-7 and MCF-10A cells incubated with CPDs-PP and siramesine. scale bars: 10 µm. Data are presented as the mean ± SD (n = 3 per group). (C) TEM images and enlarged lysosomes of MDA-MB-231 and MCF-10A cells incubated with CPDs-PP. Red arrows indicate the disruption of membrane continuity. (D) Western blot detection of cytoplasmic CTSD and whole CTSD expression levels in MDA-MB-231 and MCF-10A cells at 2, 4, 6 and 12 h after CPDs-PP treatment. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Lysosomal positioning change caused by CPDs-P. Fluorescence images of MDA-MB-231, MCF-7 and MCF-10A cells incubated with CPDs-PP for 3 h and 6 h, and labeled with lysoTracker Red for lysosomes and with DAPI for nucleus. Scale bars: 10 µm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Differential lipid species and lipid metabolism. (A) Differential lipid metabolites represented by different dots between CPDs-PP group and control group. (B) The top 20 enriched KEGG pathways of differential lipid metabolites. (C) Lipid hydrolase activity of aSMase and PLA2. (D) Schematic diagram of lysosomal lipid accumulation and LMP induced by CPDs-PP. (E) The cluster analysis graph of PE species between the CPDs-PP and the control group. (F) Schematic diagram of the autophagy KEGG pathway of differential PE species.

Autophagic flux block induced by CPDs-P. (A) Western blot analysis of LC3-I to LC3-II conversion and p62 protein levels in MCF-7 and MCF-10A cells treated with CPDs-PP/CQ/DOX. (B) Western blot analysis of time-course p62 expression levels in MCF-7 and MCF-10A cells treated with CPDs-PP. (C) TEM images and enlarged autolysosomes/autophagic vesicles of MDA-MB-231, MCF-7 and MCF-10A cells treated with CPDs-PP. Red arrows indicate MLBs. Blue arrows indicate autophagic vesicles with double membrane. (D) Schematic diagram of CPDs-PP-induced autophagic degradation blockage in cancer cells. (E) TEM images of compromised lysosomal membrane integrity and exocytosis. The yellow arrow indicates the clearance of CPDs-PP through exocytosis in MCF-10A cells. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Killing effects of CPDs-PP and photodynamic therapy on cancer cells. Calcein-AM (green, live cells)/PI (red, dead cells) double staining images of cells treated with (A) different concentration of CPDs-PP with/without Pepstain A, (C) 2 mg/mL of CPDs-PP or 50 μg/mL of PpIX under different irradiation intensity. scale bars: 50 µm. (B) Cell viability of MCF-7 cells following treatment by CPDs-PP with/without Pepstain A. (D) Cell viability of cells following treatment by 2 mg/mL of CPDs-PP or 50 μg/mL PpIX under different irradiation intensity. (E) Western blot analysis of LC3-I to LC3-II conversion and p62 protein levels in MCF-7 and MCF-10A cells treated with/without CPDs-PP and irradiation. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Inhibition effects of CPDs-PP and photodynamic therapy on tumor sphere. (A) Schematic diagram co-cultivation of tumor sphere and adherent cells. (B) FRET images of MCF-7 spheres and adherent MCF-10A cells incubated with CPDs-PP for three days and irradiated with laser. Scale bars: 200 µm. (C) Microscope images of MCF-7 spheres treated with CPDs-PP/irradiation for different durations. Scale bars: 200 µm. (D) Changes in MCF-7 spheres volume over CPDs-PP/irradiation treatment time. Data are presented as the mean ± SD (n = 3 per group).

Preparation of CPDs-PP and schematic illustration of the tumor therapy. CPDs-PP's distinct surface group design enabled it to undergo protonation and aggregation within the acidic environment of lysosomes in cancer cells, inducing lysosomal membrane permeability. This, in turn, hindered the flow of autophagy and combined with photodynamic therapy to kill tumor cells. While in normal cells, lysosomal trapping of CPDs-PP induced the autophagic degradation of lysosome to maintain cellular homeostasis, followed by the clearance of these nanoparticles through exocytosis.

head.ce:title: Surface charge accumulation of functionalized carbonized polymer dots selectively induces lysosomal membrane permeabilization of breast cancer cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lysosomal membrane permeabilization (LMP) has become an attractive strategy in tumor therapy. However, non-specific cytotoxicity caused by LMP remains a challenge; whether lysosomal-dependent autophagy process affected by lysosomal damage is still unclear. Here, we provide a carbonized polymer dots (CPDs)-based lysosomal targeted nano-strategy that mediates specific cytotoxicity to breast cancer cells by inducing LMP and disruption of autophagy degradation. CPDs functionalized via covalent conjugation with PpIX modified cathepsin D (CTSD) specific substrate peptide (CPDs-PP) were designed to achieve the selectivity to breast cancer cells. The effects and underlying mechanism of CPDs-PP on lysosomal membrane integrity and autophagic flux were determined. CPDs-PP with pH-dependent protonation and CTSD-responsive surface positive charge accumulation property at specific pH were obtained. Conversion of CPDs-PP to CPDs-P and ratiometric green–red fluorescence switching were observed in breast cancer cells with high expression of CTSD. It has been confirmed that CPDs-PP induced LMP and autophagic flux blockage of breast cancer cells. Lysosomal lipid accumulation was demonstrated to be the potential mechanism. CPDs-PP-induced LMP exhibited selective cytotoxicity to adherent tumor cells and tumor spheres, which was furtherly enhanced by spatiotemporal controlled lysosomal specific photodamage. This strategy offers promise for utilization of lysosome-targeting nanocarriers for anti-cancer pro-drugs and drug delivery systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lysosome membranes of tumor cells are more susceptible to permeabilization, leading to lysosome-dependent cell death, as compared to normal cells [1–3]. Consequently, inducing lysosomal membrane permeabilization (LMP) has demonstrated significant therapeutic effects on some cancers that are resistant to conventional therapies [4,5]. Nanocarriers are inevitably sequestered by endosomes/lysosomes, following cellular internalization [6–8]. Generally, design of a nanocarrier with the capability of lysosomal escape is necessary to prevent degradation by lysosomal hydrolases. Conversely, utilizing lysosomal trapping of nanocarriers with the intention of inducing lysosomal membrane damage and consequently triggering lysosome-dependent cell death, offers a promising strategy for anticancer treatment.

So far, various nanocarriers have been developed to induce LMP in response to external stimuli and lysosomal environment, thus to directly kill tumor cells [9,10]. For example, iron oxide nanoparticles responding to exogenous magnetism cause mechanical shear force [11,12] or local heat [13] to disrupt the lysosomal membrane. Mixed-charge gold nanoparticles [14], PD(OH)2 nanodot-confined hollow silica nanospheres [15] and selenium–rubyrin-loaded nanoparticles [16], etc, featuring lysosomal pH dependence have been developed for induction of LMP through inducing lysosomal swelling, destroying the acidic environment and generation of singlet oxygen, respectively. Also, nanocarriers composed of polypeptide amphiphilic molecules or of cationic amphiphilic drugs [17] can cause lipid peroxidation of lysosomal membrane, thereby inducing LMP.

The main objective of designing LMP-induced nanocarriers is to minimize non-specific cytotoxicity and enhance selectivity towards cancer cells. Several nanocarriers targeting the specific tumor antigen epitopes and receptors on cellular membrane surface have been shown to improve the targeting effectiveness of tumor tissue. Such as, integrin receptor αvβ3 [18] cholecystokinin-2 receptor (CCK2R), [19,20] folic acid (FA) receptor [21], human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2) [22], etc, have been used as the active targeting sites to enhance the tumor cellular internalization efficiency of the nanocarriers, which would eventually lead to LMP. However, they cannot completely eliminate the side effects caused by off-target effects. Also, pH-responsive passive targeting strategies [6,23] face the severe non-specificity issue that is the limited difference of lysosome pH between tumor and normal cells. Besides, researchers have developed many nanocarriers in response of lysosomal hydrolases, such as esterase [24], cathepsin B and D [25] and lysosomal protein marker LAMP1, which are common tumor markers, to activate drug in cancer therapeutic. This strategy can effectively reduce the off-target risks and enhance tumor-targeting selectivity. Another challenging problem is LMP could potentially affect autophagic flux which refers to the whole process of autophagy. Generally, nanoparticle-mediated autophagy plays a cytoprotective role. However, due to the involvement of lysosomes in the downstream steps of autophagy pathway [26], nanocarriers-induced lysosomal dysfunction could impair the autophagy degradation, that may result in disruption of autophagic flux and severe cytotoxicity [27]. As far as we know, the effects of LMP on the whole process of nanocarriers-mediated autophagy has not been investigated. Focusing on the autophagy initiation, without looking at the subsequent process, including autophagy-lysosome fusion and autolysosome degradation, is clearly misleading. Because autophagy blockade is likely to cause disruption of intracellular homeostasis [28].

Based on the above points, carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) were functionalized to induce LMP and the related autophagy pathway was investigated. As shown in Scheme 1 , to achieve the selectivity to breast cancer cells, CPDs were covalently conjugated with PpIX modified CTSD specific substrate peptide to obtain CPDs-PP. CTSD-responsive aggregation property, surface charge change, and fluorescence conversion of CPDs-PP in pH 4.5 solution and breast cancer cell lysosomes were investigated. Then, we evaluated if CPDs-PP could cause the loss of lysosomal membrane integrity and thus disruption of autophagic flux in breast cancer cells. Additionally, the potential mechanism for LMP induced by CPDs-PP was determined by lipidomics analysis. Finally, we confirmed the synergistic cytotoxicity effect of CPDs-PP-induced LMP and photodamage on adherent tumor cells and tumor spheres.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CTSD-responsive and pH-dependent aggregation of CPDs-PP
ce:section.ce:para: Interactions among nanoparticles can be facilitated by functional modification with proton acceptors and proton donors. These functional groups enable the nanoparticles to undergo protonation or deprotonation in response to changes in pH, ultimately forming aggregates [29,30]. Here, carboxylic-containing citric acid (CA) and amino-containing polyetherimide (PEI), with mass ratios of 20:9, 21:8 and 22:7, and fixed amount of polyethylene glycol (PEG) were first used as the synthesis precursors of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs). These CPDs all exhibited the spherical and monodispersed morphologies (Fig. 1 A). The full-scan XPS survey spectrum showed three typical peaks for each sample, corresponding to C (532 eV), N (400 eV) and O (285 eV) (Fig. S1). The high-resolution C 1 s and N 1 s spectrum showed that the amounts of carboxylic groups increased, however pyridine N decreased, with the increase of the ratio of CA/PEI (Fig. 1B and 1C). Thus, CPDs with carboxylic groups, pyridinic N and amino N, which could be protonated and proportionally controlled, were obtained.

The surface charge of CPDs at different pH were measured to provide information on the extent of protonation of the surface groups. As shown in Fig. 1D, CPDs presented increase of the surface charge with an increasing pH level. At pH = 7.5, CPDs presented negative surface charge due to deprotonation of carboxylic acid groups [31] and were well dispersed. With the decrease of pH, carboxyl, pyridine N and amino N could be protonated and thus the surface negative charge decreased whereas the positive charge increased, until the net charge was close to zero. Thus, the change of surface net charge followed the same trend of the extent of protonation as a function of pH: the greater potential zeta value, the higher degree of protonation. The hydrodynamic radius of the CPDs at different pH were measured by DLS (Fig. 1E). All the CPDs were unaggregated at pH 7.5, but had different degrees of aggregation as pH decreased. Especially, the obvious aggregated clusters with the diameter of over 200 nm was first detected in CPDs21:8 at pH 4.5 (within the pH 4 ∼ 5 of cellular lysosome). However, the pH value of the first obvious aggregation of CPDs20:9 and CPDs22:7 was lower and higher than that of CPDs21:8, respectively.

Fig. 1F schematically illustrated the structure of CPDs and the mechanism of pH-dependent protonation and surface positive charge accumulation caused by CPDs aggregation. CPDs showed carbonization core and polymer chains. The carbonization core showed highly crosslinked nitrogen-containing heterocyclic ring network structure generated by dehydration and carbonization [32,33]. Polymer chains retained partial structure of polymer precursors owing to incomplete carbonization, possessing carboxylic groups (O = C-O), pyridine N (=N-) and amino groups (–NH2). Electrostatic repulsion and hydrogen-bonding attraction were the determinants of stability of CPDs solutions [34,35]. With variation of pH, the alteration of these noncovalent interactions among particles was a result of proportion of the functional groups. The carboxylic groups were to a high degree deprotonated (–CO2 -) and negatively charged at pH 7.5, and served to intermolecular repulsion. As pH decreased, protonation of –CO2 -, =N- and –NH2 leaded to the increase of intraparticle and interparticle hydrogen bonding interactions between –COOH and = NH+-/–NH3 + [36,37]. As complete protonation of –CO2 -, hydrogen bonding attraction reached the maximum, resulting in particles aggregation and surface positive charge accumulation. Because CPDs20:9 possessed less –CO2 - and more = N-, higher protonation degree and stronger hydrogen bonding could form only at higher pH and vice versa [38].

CTSD is a lysosomal protease and a marker of poor prognosis in breast cancer [39–41]. The human protease profile database（https://www.proteinatlas.org）has reported the expression of CTSD in breast cancer tissue is markedly higher than that in normal breast epithelial tissue and in other tissues. In order to specifically respond to breast cancer cells, CPDs21:8 was further functionalized via covalent conjugation with PpIX modified CTSD specific substrate peptide (FAAFFVLC) (P-PpIX) (Fig. 2 A and Fig. S2, S3). The similar shape and larger size of the peptide-functionalized CPDs21:8 (CPDs-PP) was observed by TEM as shown in Fig. 1A. The absorption peaks at 1280 cm−1 (the stretching vibration of the C-N bond) and 1654 cm−1 (the stretching vibration of the C = O bond) in the FTIR spectrum attributed to the vibrational absorption band of CO = NH. The CO = NH characteristic peaks of CPDs-PP were significantly higher than that of CPDs21:8, confirming successful introduction of P-PpIX on the surface of CPDs21:8 (Fig. S4A).

CPDs-PP showed lower aggregation degree with decreasing pH compared with CPDs21:8 (Fig. 2B and Fig. S4B). However, CPDs-P, the digestion products of CPDs-PP by CTSD, exhibited more and more obvious aggregation with decreasing pH from 7.5 to 4.5 (Fig. 2B, 2C and S4B) [42,43]. Unlike CPDs-PP, the UV–vis absorption spectra of CPDs-P showed a gradual red shift from 348 nm to 370 nm and the maximal photoluminescence (PL) intensity decreased, as pH decreased (Fig. S4C and D). This phenomenon of maximal absorption peak red shift caused by aggregation of nanoparticles has been documented by previous study [44]. As pH value decreased, zeta potential of CPDs-PP and CPDs-P both increased gradually. At pH 4.5, the positive charge value of CPDs-P was significantly higher than that of CPDs-PP, that also may be caused by nanoparticles accumulation (Fig. 2D).

As schemed in Fig. 2E, the different distribution characteristics between CPDs-PP and CPDs-P may be attributed to π-π stacking interaction of aromatic groups in PpIX [45]. It has been reported that aromatic stacking arrangement has the ability to increase intramolecular hydrogen bonding [46,47]. Therefore, the enhanced intraparticle hydrogen bonding may weaken the interparticle hydrogen bonding interactions, resulting in high mono dispersity of CPDs-PP. After digested by CTSD, PpIX was cut off and CPDs-P would aggregate at pH = 4.5. Overall, CPDs-PP with pH-dependent protonation and CTSD-responsive surface positive charge accumulation property at specific pH were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CTSD stimuli-responsive fluorescence conversion of CPDs-PP
ce:section.ce:para: The overlap between fluorescence emission spectrum of CPDs21:8 and absorption spectrum of PpIX was observed (Fig. 3 A). It can be inferred effective energy transfer from CPDs21:8 to PpIX [48,49]. This suggested that CPDs21:8 and PplX can be used as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) molecular pairs. Under the excitation wavelength of 400 nm, the maximum emission peak appeared at 490 nm for CPDs21:8 (Fig. S5A). When excited at 400 nm, CPDs-PP showed red fluorescence and the fluorescence emission spectrum exhibited the typical PplX emission peak at 630 nm and no obvious CPDs21:8 emission peak at 490 nm (Fig. 3B and F). It confirmed the occurrence of FRET between CPDs21:8 and PpIX. As shown in Fig. S5B, various metal ions did not affect the fluorescence intensity of CPDs-PP. When exposure to CTSD, CPDs-PP demonstrated dual emission band (Fig. 3C and F). The relative intensities of the dual emission could be modulated by concentration of CTSD from 0.1 to 3.5 μg/mL significant linear association existed between CTSD concentration and the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 490 nm to 630 nm (Fig. 3D). When exposure to high concentration of CTSD at 10 μg/mL and removal of PplX. CPDs-P was obtained and emitted cyan fluorescence at 490 nm, indicating the complete cleavage of CPDs-PP and the fluorescence recovery of CPDs21:8 (Fig. 3E and F). In addition, it was found that CTSD analogues, CTSB, CTSL, CTSK and CTSS, and CTSD plus its inhibitor Pepstain A did not cause any fluorescence conversion of CPDs-PP (Fig. 3G), suggesting that the fluorescence conversion of CPDs-PP was specifically responsive to CTSD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conversion of CPDs-PP to CPDs-P and fluorescence switching in breast cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: Due to its specific fluorescence response to CTSD, the conversion and FRET imaging of CPDs-PP in cells with different levels of CTSD expression were determined. The expression of mature CTSD in cancer cells MDA-MB-231 and MCF-7 was found to be significantly higher compared to that in breast epithelial cells MCF-10A [50–52], as confirmed by Western blot analysis and Image J quantification (Fig. 4 A and 4B). Fig. 4C showed that the two cancer cells with high CTSD expression exhibited strong green fluorescence and weak red fluorescence, indicating that a major proportion of CPDs-PP was converted to CPDs-P. Conversely, MCF-10A cells with low CTSD expression displayed weak green fluorescence and strong red fluorescence, indicating a lower conversion of CPDs-PP. Quantitative analysis of pseudo-color images showed that the ratio of green to red fluorescence intensity (FG/R) of MDA-MB-231 and MCF-7 cells were both significantly higher than that of MCF-10A (Fig. 4C and Fig. S6). CPDs-PP exhibited similar fluorescence emission in other cells with high CTSD expression (Hela, Siha, HCF-116 and Huh7) and these with relatively low CTSD expression (HCAEC, ZREC-19, HUAEC and 293 T) (Fig. S7). In cancer cells pre-treated with the CTSD inhibitor Pepstain A at the concentration of 10.0 μg/mL for 8 h, the green fluorescence significantly decreased, while the red fluorescence noticeably increased, confirming the significance of CTSD stimuli in CPDs-PP fluorescence conversion.

To ensure that the response to cancer cells was applicable to the entire cell population, the fluorescence intensities of a large cell population were measured in the green and red channels using flow cytometry. As depicted in Fig. 4D, breast cancer cells exhibited strong green fluorescence, while breast epithelial cells showed strong red fluorescence. These findings suggested that CPDs-PP responded to the high expression of CTSD in breast cancer cells, leading to its conversion to CPDs-P and exhibiting ratiometric green–red fluorescence switching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CPDs-P aggregate then escape from lysosome in breast cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: The above DLS test stated that CPDs-P aggregated at pH 4.5, which is within the typical pH range of the lysosome lumen (4.5–5.0) [53,54]. Thus, the distribution of CPDs-P in cellular lysosomes was observed by TEM. TEM observation of lysosomes in breast cancer cells revealed a significant aggregation of CPDs-P, whereas no obvious aggregation was observed in normal cells (Fig. 5 A). Furthermore, fluorescent imaging demonstrated that lysosomes in cancer cells swelled as the incubation time with CPDs-PP prolonged (Fig. 5B and Fig. S8). This may be caused by the protonation of large amount of CPDs-P, which led to the influx of Cl- and H2O into the lysosomes [55], resulting in increased osmolarity and subsequent swelling (Fig. 5C).

Furtherly, the subcellular distribution of CPDs-P over time was verified. Weak red fluorescence emitted from unreacted CPDs-PP and strong green fluorescence emitted from CPDs-P were observed in both MDA-MB-231 and MCF-7 cells (Fig. 5D and Fig. S9). Importantly, after 6 h of incubation, cytoplasmic green fluorescence was significantly enhanced. And the Pearson correlation coefficient with the co-localization image of CPDs-P green fluorescence and lysosomal blue fluorescence decreased over time. This suggested that CPDs-P gradually released from lysosomes into the cytoplasm in cancer cells. In contrast, MCF-10A cells exhibited weak green fluorescence, and no cytoplasmic green fluorescence was observed after 6 h. These findings suggested that the lysosomal membrane of cancer cells may be damaged, leading to the leakage of lysosomal contents. The perturbation of membrane integrity and the leakage of lysosomal contents are hallmark characteristics of lysosomal membrane permeabilization (LMP) [56,57]. Overall, these results suggested a potential mechanism of CPDs-P protonation and positive charge accumulation involving in LMP in cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CPDs-P cause LMP of breast cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the effect of CPDs-P on the lysosomal membrane integrity, MDA-MB-231 and MCF-7 cells were stained by AO, a dye that selectively accumulates in acidic organelles and emits red fluorescence. When the lysosomal membrane is impaired, AO enters into neutral cytoplasm and emits green fluorescence [58]. Fluorescent images showed that cancer cells co-incubated with CPDs-PP and AO emitted stronger cytoplasmic green fluorescence compared to cells incubated with CPDs-PP alone. Furthermore, these cells emitted weaker lysosomal red fluorescence compared to cells incubated with AO alone (Fig. 6 A and B). This fluorescence pattern was similar to that observed in cancer cells co-incubated with AO and siramisine, a lysosome disruptor known to induce LMP. Notably, normal cells did not exhibit an increase in cytoplasmic green fluorescence or a decrease in lysosomal red fluorescence. TEM images also supported these findings, showing an increase in lysosomal diameter, and disruption of membrane continuity of cancer cell lysosomes containing a large number of nanoparticle aggregates, while normal cells maintained with lysosomal membrane continuity (Fig. 6C). These results led us to concluded that CPDs-P protonation and aggregation specifically induced LMP in breast cancer cells.

LMP initially allows the passage of small molecules like ions, eventually leading to the release of lysosomal proteases from the lysosomal lumen into the cytosolic compartments [57]. To examine the release of lysosomal CTSD, whole cell extracts and the cytoplasmic fractions from CPDs-PP incubated cells were analyzed using Western Blot (Fig. 6D, Fig. S10). The expression level of mature CTSD in the whole MDA-MB-231 and MCF-10A cells remained unchanged. Notably, cytoplasmic mature CTSD was almost undetectable in MCF-10A cells. However, in NDA-MB-231 cells, cytoplasmic mature CTSD could not be detected within 4 h of CPDs-PP treatment, but significantly increased after 6 h. The release of Lysosomal CTSD further supported the occurrence of LMP of breast cancer cells.

In addition, fluorescence imaging (Fig. 7 A–C) showed that lysosomes shifted from scattered distribution in the cytoplasm to clustered distribution around the nucleus in MDA-MB-231, and MCF-7 cells incubated with CPDs-PP. This result is consistent with that obtained by previous studies [58,59], showing that LMP might be accompanied by abnormal lysosomal positioning.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lysosomal lipid accumulation is the potential mechanism for CPDs-P-induced LMP of breast cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that cationic amphiphilic drugs (CADs) converging in lysosomes can decrease hydrolytic activity by reducing the binding sites of intralumenal vesicles (ILVs) of lipid hydrolases [60,61]. The decrease in hydrolytic activity causes lipid accumulation in lysosomes and alteration of the lysosomal membrane composition which ultimately induce LMP [62,63]. Given that CPDs-P shares structural similarities with CADs, such as hydrophobic carbonization core and hydrophilic functional groups [64,65], it is speculated that CPDs-P may also induce LMP through lysosomal lipid accumulation.

To assess the impact of CPDs-P on lysosomal lipid accumulation, lipidomics analysis was conducted to characterize the lipid metabolism profile of MCF-7 cells. A total of 5 differential lipid classes, 46 differential lipid subclasses and 1172 differential lipid metabolites were identified between the CPDs-PP group and the control group based on VIP value > 1 of the OPLS-DA models, P < 0.05 and FC > 1.01 or FC < 0.99 (Fig. S11). Among them, 361 lipid metabolites were downregulated, and 131 were upregulated in the CPDs-PP group (Fig. 8 A). In the KEGG pathway enrichment analysis [66], the top 20 enriched pathways were examined (Fig. 8B). The results revealed that these differential metabolites were mainly enriched in pathways related to cancer overview, nervous system, lipid metabolism, signal transduction, endocrine system, transport and catabolism. Specifically, the sphingolipids and glycerophospholipids metabolism pathways [67,68] showed significant enrichment of Sphingomyelin (SM), ceramide (Cer), and phosphatidylcholine (PC) metabolite species. Previous studies have associated these lipid metabolites with LMP [69,70]. Changes in the lipid composition of lysosomal membrane, influenced by these lipid metabolites, can potentially decrease membrane integrity and lead to LMP [49]. As shown in Fig. 8A, the counts of up-/down-regulated lipid species within SM, Cer and PC subclasses were 16/2, 87/4 and 64/31, respectively. Additionally, the clustering heatmap (Fig. S12) indicated that the majority of differential lipid species within SM, Cer and PC subclasses were significantly up-regulated in CPDs-PP group compared to the control group. Therefore, the accumulation of SM, Cer and PC were identified as the main influencing factors contributing to LMP in CPDs-PP treated MCF-7 cells.

Lipid accumulation is commonly attributed to a decrease in lipid hydrolase activity [70,71]. Acid sphingomyelinase (aSMase) [72–74] and phospholipase A2 (PLA2) [75,76], which are responsible for the hydrolysis of SM and PC, were investigated for their activity. The results revealed a gradually decrease in the activity of both hydrolases in MCF-7 cells as the concentration of CPDs-PP increased (Fig. 8C). These hydrolases typically bind to negatively charged acidic ILVs and maintain their activity [37]. Previous studies have reported that protonated CADs compete with PLA2, aSMase and aCDsase (acid ceramidase) for ILVs binding, leading to a decline in hydrolase activity. Therefore, it is inferred that protonated CPDs-P may also inhibit the activity of PLA2, aSMase and aCDsase by competitively binding to ILVs and cause lysosomal PC, SM and Cer accumulation, ultimately resulting in LMP (Fig. 8D).

Autophagy is an evolutionarily conserved degradation system of damaged organelle. It has been observed that selective autophagy can effectively clear dysfunctional lysosomes [77]. As shown in Fig. 8B, autophagy pathway was one of the top 20 enriched KEGG pathways and only seven phosphatidyl ethanolamine (PE) species were enriched in it. Cluster analysis revealed that these PE species were all up-regulated in the CPDs-PP groups (Fig. 8E). The cysteine protease ATG4 cleaves LC3 into LC3-I, which is then conjugated to PE with the help of ATG3 and ATG7, to form LC3-I-PE (LC3-II). LC3-II is subsequently recruited to autophagosome membranes, directly contributing in autophagosome elongation and maturation [78]. In the autophagy KEGG pathway (Fig. 8F), the up-regulated PE species induced LC3-II accumulation, indicating the promotion of autophagosome formation by LC3 lipidation, as supported by previous studies [79,80]. Thus, it is possible that CPDs-P-induced up-regulation of PE in MCF-7 cells activated protective autophagy to remove dysfunctional lysosomes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CPDs-P inhibit lysosomal autophagy degradation in breast cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: Given the role of autophagy in clearing dysfunctional organelles, autophagic flux of CPDs-PP treated cells was assessed by western-blot. LC3 conversion (LC3-I to LC3-II) and the autophagy-dependent degradation substrates P62 in the absence and presence of autophagy inducer (Dox) and inhibitor (CQ, impairing autophagosome-lysosome fusion) was examined. The findings revealed that the levels of LC3-II were enhanced in both MCF-7 and MCF-10A cells treated with CPDs-PP (Fig. 9 A). This indicates that CPDs-P activates the formation of autophagosome in both cancer and normal cells. In the presence of CQ, the amount of LC3-II remains constant when autophagy flux is blocked. Conversely, when autophagy flux is taking place, the amount of LC3-II is increased. In MCF-10A cells, the LC3-II levels in the CPDs-PP + CQ group were higher compared to the CPDs-PP group. However, in MCF-7 cells, both groups showed equal levels of LC3-II. This implies that CPDs-P are able to block autophagy flux in breast cancer cells.

As an indicator of autophagic flux, the expression of the autophagy substrate p62 protein is inversely related with autophagic degradation. Lower levels of p62 were observed in both normal and cancer cells treated with Dox, as well as in normal cells treated with CPDs-PP (Fig. 9A, S13), indicating enhanced autophagic flux and degradation of p62. However, in MCF-7 cancer cells, CPDs-PP treatment resulted in higher levels of p62, demonstrating blockage of autophagic flux and accumulation of autophagosomes. In MCF-10A cells, p62 levels gradually decreased over time after CPDs-PP treatment (Fig. 9B, Fig. S14), indicating accelerated autophagic flux. However, in MDA-MB-231, CPDs-PP treatment initially led to decreased p62 levels within the first 2 h, followed by increased levels afterwards. This suggests that CPDs-PP blocked autophagic flux at the stage of autophagosome-lysosome fusion and autolysosome degradation. Overall, these results indicate that CPDs-PP induced autophagosome formation in both cancer and normal cells, but specifically suppressed autophagic degradation in cancer cells.

To further confirm the effect of CPDs-PP on autophagy, TEM was performed to determine the formation of autophagic vesicles in CPDs-PP treated cancer and normal cells. In MCF-10A cells, autophagic vesicles containing uniformly distributed CPDs-PP displayed a non-lamellar structure with double membrane. However, in MDA-MB-231 and MCF-7 cells, multilamellar bodies (MLB) were observed in autolysosomes which contained clusters of accumulated CPDs-P (Fig. 9C). MLB is a common characteristic observed in autophagic vacuole when lysosomal degradation is blocked [64,81]. Lysosomal lipids accumulation could be the cause of the formation of multilayer membrane structures of MLB [82–84].

The lysosome is a critical juncture between protective autophagy and lysosomal cell death [85]. As schemed in Fig. 9D, lysosomal trapping of CPDs-PP induced autophagosome formation either in cancer and normal cells. Especially, in cancer cells, LC3-I-PE conjugate (LC3-II) recruited to the autophagosomal membrane and induced autophagosome formation. However, the autophagic degradation of lysosome was blocked in cancer cells. There may be two reasons, first, defective lysosomal degradative capacity caused by lysosomal proteases lacking, particularly CTSD [86]. Second, the alteration of lysosomal membrane composition may affect autophagosome-lysosome fusion [87]. Taken together, in cancer cells, CPDs-P induced the irreparable lysosomal membrane damage and abrogated the ability of cells to complete autophagic responses, thereby leading to lysosome-dependent cell death. While in normal cells, lysosomal trapping of CPDs-PP induced the autophagic degradation of lysosome to maintain cellular homeostasis, followed by the clearance of these nanoparticles through exocytosis (Fig. 8E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergistic induction of cancer cell death by CPDs-PP treatment and photodynamic therapy
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the crucial role of lysosomal integrity and function in cell homeostasis, LMP can lead to lysosome-dependent cell death by releasing lysosomal hydrolases into the cytoplasm [88]. Consequently, it is plausible that LMP induction by CPDs-PP would affect the survival of cancer cells. Calcein-AM/PI double staining and CCK8 assay (Fig. 10 A and B) demonstrated the cytotoxicity of CPDs-PP in a concentration-dependent manner on cancer MCF-7 cells, that could be mitigated by the protease inhibitor Pepstain A. However, CPDs-PP did not exhibit any toxic effects on normal MCF-10A cells. These findings indicate the selective killing effects of CPDs-PP on breast cancer cells.

To enhance the cytotoxicity of CPDs-PP, cells were irradiated with an 808 nm laser for 6 min to activate the photosensitizer PpIX released form CPDs-PP hydrolysis and generate reactive oxygen species (ROS). When MCF-7 cells treated with 2.0 mg/mL CPDs-PP were irradiated, their lethality and ROS production (Fig. S15) was significantly higher compared to cells without irradiation and PpIX treated cells (Fig. 10C and 10D). This suggests that the combined effect of CPDs-PP and photodynamic irradiation increased the cytotoxicity to breast cancer cells. However, under the same conditions, CPDs-PP had almost no phototoxicity to MCF-10A cells. Increased LC3-II (Fig. 10E and Fig. S16B) revealed that autophagic flux was activated in normal cells, that may be to remove lysosomes containing CPDs-PP. Furtherly increased LC3-II and decreased P62 indicated that adjusting the illumination pattern, duration and intensity enhanced the autophagic flux (Fig. 10E and S16B,D) [89]. This could be attributed to spatiotemporal controlled lysosomal specific photodamage [90,91], which enhanced protective autophagy and reduced the adverse effects of lysosomal minor damage in normal epithelial cells [92]. In contrast, increased LC3-II and decreased P62 were observed in cancer cells, whether they were illuminated or not，indicated the autophagic flux impairment (Fig. 10E and Fig. S16A, C). This indicates that the damaged lysosomes in cancer cells were unable to be incorporated into autolysosomes for degradation. Overall, these results suggest that CPDs-PP-induced LMP, in combination with ROS produced by photodamage, specifically increases the cancer cell death efficiency.

Compared to traditional two-dimensional cell culture monolayers, tridimensional (3D) cell models, such as spheroids, are able to accurately simulate the features of solid tumors, including spatial architecture and physiological responses [93]. To investigate the impact of CPDs-PP on solid tumors, MCF-7 cells were cultured in 3DmatrilGel for two weeks, resulting in the formation of compact MCF-7 tumor spheres. The spheres were then co-cultured with adherent MCF-10A cells (Fig. 11 A). FRET images demonstrated that the MCF-7 cell sphere exhibited strong green fluorescence, while the surrounding MCF-10A cells exhibited strong red fluorescence (Fig. 11B), consistent with the different fluorescence exhibited by adherent MCF-7 and MCF-10A cells. Furthermore, the tumor sphere size of 5 days after CPDs-PP treatment decreased by 72.5 % (Fig. 11C and 11D). When illuminated with 868 nm laser, the tumor size of CPDs-PP treated group decreased by more than 88.8 % after 5 days. These findings suggest that the combination of LMP and PDT could effectively inhibit the growth of breast tumor cell spheres, thereby offering promise for breast cancer therapy.

The data presented here clearly demonstrate that CPDs-PP are capable of inducing LMP and disruption of protective autophagy degradation to exert specific antitumor effect. This LMP-based nano-strategy overcoming the off-target effects and non-specificity offers a promising prospect for anti-breast cancer treatment. pH-dependent protonation and CTSD-responsive surface positive charge accumulation at specific pH are the basis of CPDs-PP to induce the loss of lysosomal membrane integrity. Importantly, our data also indicate that CPDs-PP specifically suppress autophagic degradation in cancer cells, potentially leading to disruption of intracellular homeostasis. While in normal cells, lysosomal trapping of CPDs-PP induced the autophagic degradation of lysosome to maintain cellular homeostasis, followed by the clearance of these nanoparticles through exocytosis. Moreover, CPDs-PP-induced LMP, in combination with ROS produced by photodamage, specifically increases the cancer cell death efficiency. These data support the rational design of lysosome-targeting nanocarriers to induce LMP and disrupt autophagy degradation with the aim to identify and kill cancer cells. To further validate these findings, we plan to extend the research to breast cancer of mouse model. Also, similar design can be applied to develop polymer-based particles or dendritic macromolecules with pH-dependent protonation and surface charge accumulation property at specific pH.

Poly ethylenimine (PEI, 25 kDa), polyethylene glycol (PEG, 4 kDa) and citric acid (CA) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Typsin, LysoTracker Red, LysoBrite Blue and 4′, 6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) were obtained from Gibco BRL (CA, USA). Living cell lysosomal membrane permeability acridine orange (AO) fluorescence detection kit was purchased from Hepeng Bio (Shanghai, China). Calcein-AM/PI Cell Double Staining Kit and Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) were purchased from Beyotime (Shanghai, China). DHE-ROS reactive oxygen species detection kit (BB-47051) was purchased from Bestbio (Nanjing, China). RIPA lysis buffer was purchased from Minibio (M1007). SDS-PAGE gel preparation kit and BCA Protein Quantitative Kit were purchased from Saiwen Biotech (Beijing, China). Acid Recombinant Human Cathepsin D (CTSD, ab307480), Cathepsin B (CTSB, ab283434) Cathepsin K (CTSK, ab157067), Cathepsin S (CTSS, ab285884), Cathepsin L (CTSL, ab81780) Protein and recombinant anti-Cathepsin D antibody (ab75811), anti-GAPDH antibody (ab8245), anti-alpha tubulin antibody (TUBA, ab7291), anti-LC3B antibody (ab192890), anti-p62 antibody (ab109012), HRP Anti-Rabbit IgG antibody (ab288151), Phospholipase A2 Assay kit (ab133089) and Sphingomyelinase Assay kit (ab190554) were purchased from Abcam (Shanghai, China). FG Super sensitive ECL luminescence reagent was purchased from Meilunbio (MA0186-1). VitroGel™3D hydrogel was purchased from Xiaopeng Biotechnology (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and functionalization of carbonized polymer dots
ce:section.ce:para: Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method. A mixture of CA, PEI and PEG was used in ratios of 20:9:1, 21:8:1, and 22:7:1, respectively. The mixture was transferred to a reactor lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and placed it in a homogeneous reactor (GSH-100ML, Weihai, China) at 200 ℃ for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was transferred into a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO = 2 kDa) and dialyzed for 24 h to remove impurities. The dialyzed solution was concentrated and freeze-dried to obtain the three types of CPDs: CPDs20:9, CPDs21:8, CPDs22:9. These CPDs were stored away from light for further use.

CTSD specific substrate peptides, Phe-Ala-Ala-Phe-Phe-Val-Leu-Cys (FAAFFVLC) with PpIX (P-PpIX) were synthesized by Taigu Biology Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). P-PpIX was dissolved with dimethyl formamide (DMSO) to the concentration of 1.4 mg/mL, and mixed with 10 mg 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl-carbodiimide (EDC) and 10 mg N-hydroxy succinimide (NHS). Then the mixture was vortexed for 2 h to form reactive ester groups. CPDs21:8 solution with a concentration of 10 mg/mL, was added to the reaction mixture, and agitated magnetically at room temperature for 24 h. This will result in the formation of a stable amide product between the –COOH groups of P-PpIX and the –NH2 groups of CPDs21:8. After dialysis, CPDs-P-PpIX (CPDs-PP) was obtained and stored away from light for further use. CPDs-PP with concentration of 2 mg/mL, was digested by 10 μg/mL CTSD for 50 min. After dialysis (MWCO = 2 kDa) for 1 d, PpIX was removed and the dialyzed solution was concentrated and freeze-dried to obtain CPDs-P.

The morphology and particle size of CPDs, CPDs-PP and CPDs-P were determined using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan). Surface element compositions of CPDs were gained by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Scientific, USA) analysis. The mixtures of 2 mg/mL of CPDs, CPDs-PP and CPDs-P and 40 mM of Britton-Robinson buffer solution were prepared, respectively, in volume ratio of 1: 100, and then the pH was adjusted to 4.5, 5.5, 6.5 and 7.5, respectively. To examine the impact of pH on protonation, the hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential of CPDs, CPDs-PP and CPDs-P with different pH were determined using a dynamic light scattering analyzer (DLS, Zetasizer Nano S90, Malvern, France). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) were recorded using an infrared spectrometer (Tensor 27, Bruker, Germany). Ultraviolet–visible spectrometer (UV–vis, UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan) was used to measure the UV–vis absorption spectra. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded using a microplate fluorescence spectrometer (Synergy H1, Bio-Tek, USA). Each group was measured three times and the average value was taken. Three samples were set for each pH.

The total protein of the cells was extracted using RIPA lysis buffer. Cytoplasmic protein was extracted according to the protocol described in the ReadiPrep™ Nuclear/Cytoplasmic Fractionation Kit (Qiyi Biotechnology, Shanghai, China). After rinsing twice with cold PBS, 500 μL of 1X cytosol extraction buffer was added to cells. Scrape adherent cells into a 1.5 mL centrifuge tube, vortex vigorously to completely resuspend the cells. After centrifugation at 16,000 g for 2 min, the supernatant was obtained as cytoplasmic extract, keeping on ice or storing at −80 °C. The protein samples were quantified using BCA protein quantitative kit, then subjected to SDS-PAGE gel and transferred onto the PVDF membrane. The membrane was blocked in 5 % skimmed milk for 1 h at room temperature. The blots were incubated with recombinant anti-CTSD (1:1000), TUBA (1:2000), P62 (1:200), LC3 (1:3000) and GAPDH (1:5000) antibody overnight at 4 °C, samples were then incubated with horseradish-peroxidase-conjugated secondary antibody (1:2000) at room temperature for 1 h. Furtherly, the membrane was washed 3 times in tris buffer saline containing 0.1 % Tween, each time 10 min. Protein bands were visualized with ECL kit and analyzed on a Chemidoc XRS + System (Bio-Rad, USA). The intensity of the band in each sample was quantified using Quantity One software (Bio-Rad Laboratories, Inc), corrected for the intensity of the corresponding band obtained with the anti-GAPDH antibody or anti-TUBA antibody, and normalized to the control sample. All experiments were carried out independently at least 3 times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture, cytotoxicity test and fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Human triple negative breast carcinoma (MDA-MB-231) cells, human adenocarcinoma cancer (MCF-7) cells and human cervical carcinoma (Hela, Siha) cells, human colon cancer (HCF-116) cells, human hepatoma (Huh7) cells were maintained in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle medium (DMEM, Gibco). Human breast epithelial (MCF-10A) cells, human Coronary Artery Endothelial (HCAEC) cells, human umbilical artery endothelial (HUAEC) cells, adult Retinal Pigment Epithelial cell line-19 (ARPE-19) and human embryonic kidney (293T) cells were maintained in DMEM/F-12 medium (Gibco). These cells were supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS, Gibco) and 1 % penicillin–streptomycin solutions (100 U/mL penicillin and 100 μg/mL streptomycin), and cultured at 37 °C in 5 % CO2.

These cells were cultured in 96-well plates for 24 h. To observe FRET-based ratiometric fluorescence, Pepstain A was added to the cell culture fluid up to 10.0 μg/mL and treated for 12 h, followed by incubation cells with CPDs-PP up to 2 mg/mL for 2 h. To test the cytotoxicity of CPDs-PP, the post-incubated cells were doubly stained by Calcein-AM and PI according to the manufacture’s procedure. To observe lysosomes and colocalization of CPDs-PP and lysosomes, at 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 6 and 12 h post-incubation of CPDs-PP, the cells were treated with 50 nM LysoBrite Blue for 30 min to label lysosomes, then rinsed and incubated with PBS. To assess the lysosomal membrane integrity, cells were treated with 2 mg/mL of CPDs-PP for 6 h or 10 μM of Siramisine for 3 h, followed by AO staining according to the procedures of the instruction (Green channel: 488/528 nm; Red channel: 555 nm/617 nm). To observe the relative spatial distribution between lysosomes and nucleus, at 3 h, 6 h post-incubation of CPDs-PP, the cells were treated with 50 nM LysoBrite Blue for 30 min to label lysosomes and 2 μM DAPI for 10 min to stain the nucleus.

The fluorescent images were acquired using the automated microscope (Lionheart, Bio-Tek, USA) at four channels (DAPI channel: 377/447 nm; roGFP2 channel: 400/525 nm; RFP channel: 531/593 nm; FRET channel: 400/647 nm). The ratio of green to red fluorescence emission intensity (FGreen/FRed) of the whole cell (six cells per sample) was analyzed using Image J and calculated. The diameter of more than 50 nM LysoBrite Blue-labelled lysosomes of the selected cell was measured using Image J. To determine the relative spatial distribution between CPDs-P and lysosomes, Pearson’s correlation coefficient (Pr) based on the correlation between the green and blue channel pixel intensity histogram of the whole cell was measured using Image J and calculated.

Flow cytometry was performed to assess FRET in a high number of cells. At 6 h post-incubation of 2 mg/mL of CPDs-PP with/without 10.0 μg/mL of Pepstain A, the cells were trypsinized and washed with PBS not containing EDTA. FRET was determined on flow cytometry (CytoFlex LX flow cytometer, USA) using filter sets: a 405 nm laser and 450/45 filter for donor channel measurement, a 405 nm laser and 610/20 filter for FRET channel measurement. The experiments were performed in triplicate.

After exposure to 2 mg/mL of CPDs-PP for 6 h, the cells were harvested, washed with PBS and fixed with 2 % glutaraldehyde and 2 % paraformaldehyde solution in PBS-sodium cacodylate buffer, followed by post-fixation with 2 % osmium tetroxide. After staining with 2 % uranyl acetate, the cells were embedded in propylene oxide and Eponate. The ultrathin sections (80 nm thick) collected on copper grids with Formvar supporting membranes were observed by TEM (HT7800, Hitachi, Japan).

Lipid metabolomic analysis of the cell samples was conducted using a Liquid Chromatograph-Mass Spectrometry (LC-MS) platform of Majorbio Bio-Pharm Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The samples were subjected to pre-processing to remove proteins and impurities, and then the lipid metabolites were extracted. The samples were then analyzed on an LC-MS platform in both positive and negative modes to collect information. The MS and MS/MS information of the metabolites was obtained and analyzed using the software Lipidsearch (Thermo Fisher, USA). Differential lipid metabolites were selected using T-test and VIP (OPLS-DA). Further personalized analysis, including classification analysis, cluster analysis and KEGG pathway enrichment analysis, was conducted to explore the biological information of the differential lipids.

The lipid hydrolase activity of acidic sphingomyelinase and Phospholipase A2 was measured according to the protocol described in the Sphingomyelinase Assay kit and Phospholipase A2 Assay kit, respectively.

The cells were cultured in a 96-well plate and incubated with/without 10.0 μg/mL Pepstain A for 8 h, followed by incubation with 0.5, 1 and 2 mg/mL of CPDs-PP for 6 h. Post transfected cells viabilities were determined by CCK-8 assay according to the manufacturer’s protocol, with PBS cultured cells as the negative control. The absorbance at 450 nm was measured. All measurements were taken from three independent experiments. ROS detection was detected by fluorescence spectroscopy using Dihydroethidium (DHE). MCF-7 cells treated with CPDs-PP, PpIX and CPDs21:8 under different irradiation intensity was incubated with 10 μM DHE for 30 min at 37℃. The fluorescence intensity was measured at excitation wavelength of 518 nm.

The MCF-7 tumor spheres were cultured using the VitroGel ™ 3D hydrogel. About 1x106 MCF-7 cells/mL were resuspended in a medium containing 10 % FBS, the hydrogel solution was directly mixed with cell suspension at 3:1 (v/v) mixing ratio. 200 µl of the hydrogel mix was then transferred to a 6 well plate. The gel was stabilized at room temperature for 15 min before adding 2 ml µl cover medium on the top of the hydrogel, incubating at 37 ℃ for two weeks and replacing the upper culture medium every 48 h. Images were acquired with a Zeiss Axiovert 135 microscope and the diameter of spheres was determined with the use of ImageJ, then estimating the volume of tumor spheres.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodynamic treatment of cells and tumor sphere
ce:section.ce:para: MCF-7 and MCF-10A cells were treated with CPDs-PP (2 mg/ml) or PpIX (50 μg/ml) for 6 h. The Diode Laser System (excitation wavelength: 808 nm; manufacturer: BWT Beijing Ltd., Beijing, China) was used as a source for evocation of the photodynamic effects. In cell experiments, the laser was used with a power intensity of 0–100 mW/cm2 (power: 0–200 mW, Irradiation Area: 2 cm2) and an irradiation time of 360 s to achieve the final light dose. In tumor sphere experiment, the laser was used with a power intensity of 100 W/cm2(power: 0–200 mW, Irradiation Area: 2 cm2) and an irradiation time of 600 s. The generation of ROS was confirmed using ROS detection kits, incubating the DHE probe and cells together at 37 ℃ for about 30 min and then measure the absorbance. All the experiments were performed in triplicate.

Yu Lei: Writing – original draft, Validation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Bao-Hua Tian: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Xin-Xin Li: Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Ming-Yuan Sun: Visualization, Formal analysis. Xi-Lin Guo: Visualization, Investigation. Yi-Da Wang: Validation, Software. Hui-Qing Zhou: Supervision. Ri-Sheng Ma: Validation, Project administration. Hai-Xia Liang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.152710.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Transcriptional control of autophagy-lysosome function drives pancreatic cancer metabolism
Nature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.M. Perera, S. Stoykova, B.N. Nicolay, K.N. Ross, J. Fitamant, M. Boukhali, J. Lengrand, V. Deshpande, M.K. Selig, C.R. Ferrone, J. Settleman, G.
